elements.sent-2411-001: In the work [$\Oh{m\cdot n$~algorithms for the recognition and isomorphism problems on circular-arc graphs , SIAM J.. Comput.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Sci., 15(1), 157--182, 2013] Curtis, Lin, McConnell, Nussbaum, Soulignac, Spinrad, and Szwarcfiter showed that Hsu's isomorphism algorithm is incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this note, we show that the other two results -- namely, the construction of decomposition trees and the recognition algorithm -- are also flawed.
elements.sent-2411-005: Given a family $\mathcal{F}$ of sets, the intersection graph of $\mathcal{F}$ is obtained by representing every set in $\mathcal{F}$ by a vertex and connecting two vertices by an edge if and only if their corresponding sets have non-empty intersection.. A~graph~$G$ is:.
elements.sent-2411-006: In the work [\mbox{$\Oh{m\cdot n$~algorithms for the recognition and isomorphism problems on circular-arc graphs , SIAM J.. Comput.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: The first of these results claims a construction of a decomposition tree of a circular-arc graph, a data structure intended to represent the set of all its normalized intersection models (an analogue of the PQ-tree for an interval graph).
elements.sent-2411-008: Normalized intersection models (formally defined in Section~\ref{sec:preliminaries ) of a~circular-arc graph reflect the neighborhood relation between its vertices and can be seen as its canonical representations; in particular, any intersection model can be made normalized by possibly extending some of its arcs.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Based on these decomposition trees, Hsu claims $\Oh{nm}$-algorithms for both the recognition and isomorphism problem for circular-arc graphs.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this short note we show that the description of the normalized intersection models given by Hsu in~\cite{Hsu95 is not correct.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Specifically, we show the following: \begin{counterexample There are circular-arc graphs whose all normalized models do not follow the description given by Hsu.}
elements.sent-2411-012: We note that Curtis, Lin, McConnell, Nussbaum, Soulignac, Spinrad, and Szwarcfiter ~\cite{counterex13 identified a different flaw in Hsu's isomorphism algorithm already in 2013.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The approach used by Hsu (described below) determines somehow the main steps that need to be taken to describe the structure of the normalized models of a circular-arc graph.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this paper, we also identify several errors in Hsu's work, showing that these steps were either partially or entirely incorrectly accomplished.
elements.sent-2411-015: To describe the structure of the normalized models of a circular-arc graph Hsu uses a~method that has its roots in the work of Gallai.
elements.sent-2411-016: In his seminal paper~\cite{Gal67 from 1967 Gallai introduced a data structure, called modular decomposition tree, which keeps a track of all modules of a simple graph~$G$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: We recall that a set $M \subseteq V(G)$ is a module of~$G$ if every vertex from outside $M$ is either adjacent to all vertices in~$M$ or non-adjacent to all vertices in~$M$.
elements.sent-2411-018: In the same work Gallai described the structure of all transitive orientations of a comparability graphs.
elements.sent-2411-020: If $G$ is prime (contains only trivial modules, $V(G)$ and $\{v\}$ for all $v \in V(G)$), then $G$ has a unique transitive orientation (up to reversal).
elements.sent-2411-021: Otherwise, the structure of all transitive orientations of $G$ can be described (and represented) by means of the modular decomposition tree of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-022: The above-mentioned results of Gallai have turned out to be widely used to describe the structure of some geometric intersection graphs, especially those related to partial orders.
elements.sent-2411-023: Among them are, for example, permutation graphs~\cite{DM41 , interval graphs\footnote{A reader familiar with PQ-trees and modular decomposition trees might easily observe that the PQ-tree representing the structure of intersection models of an interval graph can be easily obtained from its modular decomposition tree.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In particular, Properties~\ref{prop:G1 and~\ref{prop:G2 allow to show that for every graph~$G$ from each of those graph classes, if a certain graph~$G'$ associated with~$G$ is prime\footnote{$G'=G$ if $G$ is an interval or a permutation graph, $G'$ is so-called split of $G$ if $G$ is a trapezoid graph.}}}
elements.sent-2411-025: As for circular-arc graphs, the work \cite{Spin88 of Spinrad from 1988 is the first that allows to describe, in the way given above, the structure of the normalized models of certain graphs in this class, namely those that are co-bipartite (whose vertex set can be partitioned into two cliques).}
elements.sent-2411-026: See~\cite{Kra24 where we briefly outline the influence of Spinrad's work~\cite{Spin88 on the development of the method applied in~\cite{Hsu95 and in~\cite{Kra24 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: In~\cite{Hsu95 Hsu tries to use Gallai's framework to describe the structure of the normalized models of any circular-arc graph~$G$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: For this purpose, Hsu transforms every normalized model~$R$ of~$G$ to a chord model~$D$ by converting each arc~$R(v)$ into a chord~$D(v)$ with the same endpoints as~$R(v)$, for every $v \in V(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-029: The crucial observation of Hsu is that the resulting chord model $D$ represents the same circle graph~$G_c$, regardless of a normalized model $R$ of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Then, Hsu tries to describe the structure of the chord models of $G_c$ that correspond to the normalized models of $G$ -- such models of $G_{c}$ are called conformal to~$G$ (shortly, conformal).
elements.sent-2411-031: To this end, Hsu attempts to prove the properties similar to~\ref{prop:G1 and~\ref{prop:G2 :.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: If $G_c$ is prime, then $G_c$ has a unique conformal model (up to reflection).
elements.sent-2411-033: Otherwise, the structure of the conformal models of $G_c$ can be described by means of the modular decomposition tree of $G_c$.
elements.sent-2411-034: When $G$ is co-biparite, $G_c$ is a permutation graph, and hence Property~\ref{prop:H1 follows by~\ref{prop:G1 and Property~\ref{prop:H2 follows by~\ref{prop:G2 and by the work~\cite{Spin88 of Spinrad -- see~\cite{Kra24 for more details.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: We want to emphasize that Property~\ref{prop:H1 is crucial for the method as the structure of the conformal models is ``build'' upon the unique conformal models of prime subgraphs of~$G_c$ (the role of~\ref{prop:H1 is similar to the role of the fact that every prime subgraph has a unique transitive orientation in the description of the structure of all transitive orientations of a comparability graph).}}
elements.sent-2411-036: In~Subsection~\ref{subsec:prime_overlap_graphs_and_conformal_models we show that the ``proof'' of~\ref{prop:H1 proposed by Hsu (Theorem~5.7 in~\cite{Hsu95 ) is not correct -- it is based on two claims, both of which are shown to be false in Section~\ref{sec:prime_case .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Nevertheless, the statement of Property~\ref{prop:H1 is true, which follows from the proof presented in~\cite{Kra24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: To accomplish~\ref{prop:H2 Hsu divides the description of the structure of the conformal models of $G_c$ into three cases: when $G_c$ is disconnected, when the complement $\overline{G_c}$ of $G_c$ is disconnected, and when both $G_c$ and $\overline{G_c}$ are connected.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Hsu's work~\cite{Hsu95 correctly deals with the case when $\overline{G_c}$ is disconnected (then, each component of $\overline{G_c}$ induces a co-bipartite circular-arc graph), it is incorrect when both $G_c$ and $\overline{G_c}$ are connected, and it is incomplete (and also incorrect) when $G_c$ is disconnected.}
elements.sent-2411-040: For the case when both $G_c$ and $\overline{G_c}$ are connected, Hsu first ``proves'' Property~\ref{prop:H1 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Next, Hsu tries to partition the set $V(G_c)$ into so-called consistent modules of $G_c$, which were supposed to satisfy the following property:.
elements.sent-2411-042: For every conformal model $D$ and every consistent module $M$ of $G_c$ there are two disjoint arcs $A_M$ and $B_M$ of the circle such that each arc $A_M$ and $B_M$ contains one endpoint of every chord from $D(M)$ and no endpoint of any other chord from $D(V \setminus M)$ (it means, in particular, that $G_c[M]$ is a permutation graph).
elements.sent-2411-043: Moreover, there is a unique circular order (up to reflection) in which the arcs $A_M$ and $B_M$ associated with distinct consistent modules $M$ of~$G_c$ may occur around the circle in all conformal models of~$G_c$.
elements.sent-2411-044: In Subsection~\ref{subsec:consistent_modules we show that the ``consistent modules'' determined by Hsu do not satisfy Property~\ref{prop:H3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: This enables us, in particular, to construct a circular-arc graph claimed by Counterexample~\ref{count:main_counter .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Hence, the theorems from~\cite{Hsu95 ``proving'' Property~\ref{prop:H3 for the ``consistent modules'' determined by Hsu are also not correct -- see Subsection \ref{subsec:consistent_modules for more details.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: The description of Hsu is also not correct in the case when $G_c$ is disconnected (the errors mentioned above are transferred to the description of the conformal models of some components of $G_c$).
elements.sent-2411-048: Moreover, this part of Hsu's work is missing many important ingredients (the consistent modules are not defined for all components of $G_c$) -- we refer to Section~\ref{sec:parallel_case for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Although Hsu's work \cite{Hsu95 is flawed, the approach taken in it is appropriate.}
elements.sent-2411-050: In~\cite{Kra24 we assume a different definition of conformal models, which allows us to describe the structure of all normalized models of a circular-arc graph using the same method.}
elements.sent-2411-051: We want to mention that, despite the errors, Hsu's work had a great influence on the work done in~\cite{Kra24 -- we refer to~\cite{Kra24 when we mention the ideas of Hsu used in this work.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: In particular, Hsu's ideas have significantly contributed to understanding the structure of the normalized models, even in cases where bugs were identified.
elements.sent-2411-053: In this section, we introduce the notation from Hsu's work~\cite{Hsu95 that is needed to present counterexamples to the aforementioned (false) claims made by Hsu.}
elements.sent-2411-054: For a simple graph $G$, by $V(G)$ and $E(G)$ we denote the vertex set and the edge set of $G$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-055: For $v$ in $V(G)$, we denote by $N(v)$ (or $N_G(v)$) the set consisting of $v$ and all vertices adjacent to $v$ in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-056: Vertices $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G$ are said to form a similar pair if $N(v_1) \setminus \{v_1\} = N(v_2) \setminus \{v_2\}$.
elements.sent-2411-057: A vertex $v$ in $G$ is said to be a D-vertex (short for dominating) if $N(v) = V(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-058: For a set $M \subseteq V(G)$ by $G[M]$ we denote the subgraph of~$G$ induced by the set $M$.
elements.subsection-2411-001: The modular decomposition and the join decomposition
elements.sent-2411-060: A module $M$ of $G$ is a subset of $V(G)$ such that between $M$ and each vertex $v$ in $V(G)\setminus M$ there are either no edges or all possible edges.
elements.sent-2411-061: Module $M$ is connected iff $G[M]$ is connected.
elements.sent-2411-062: Module $M$ is complement connected iff the complement of $G[M]$ is connected.
elements.sent-2411-063: An unconnected module is called a parallel module.
elements.sent-2411-064: A~connected module whose complement is unconnected is called a series module.
elements.sent-2411-065: A~module that is both connected and complement connected is called a neighborhood module.
elements.sent-2411-066: A~module $M$ of size greater than $1$ is nontrivial if $M \neq V(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-067: A~graph $G$ is s-inseparable if it does not contain any nontrivial module.
elements.sent-2411-069: A~graph $G$ which is s-inseparable is also called prime.
elements.sent-2411-070: By definition, every module is exactly one of these three types.
elements.sent-2411-071: The modular decomposition starts with the module $V = V(G)$ and creates a corresponding root node in the decomposition tree labelled with S, P, or N, depending on the type of $V$.
elements.sent-2411-072: Then it decomposes this module into components $M_1, M_2,\ldots, M_k$ using one of the following rules:.
elements.sent-2411-073: For each $M_i$, find the modular decomposition tree of~$G[M_i]$ and make its root node a son of the root node representing~$V(G)$.
elements.sent-2411-074: The resulting tree is called a modular decomposition tree of~$G$; it is proven in~\cite{Gal67 that the modular decomposition tree of~$G$ is unique.}
elements.sent-2411-076: A graph $G$ is said to have a join if $V$ can be partitioned into $V_0$, $V_1$, $V_2$, and $V_3$ with $V_0 \cup V_1 \geq 2$ and $V_2 \cup V_3 \geq 2$ such that every possible edge exists between $V_1,V_2$ and no edge exists between $V_0, V_2 \cup V_3$, or between $V_0 \cup V_1,V_3$.
elements.sent-2411-077: In this case, we say $G$ is decomposable into $H_1$ and $H_2$ where $H_1$ is the induced subgraph of $G[V_0 \cup V_1]$ with an extra vertex $v_1$ adjacent to every vertex in $V_1$ and $H_2$ is the induced subgraph of $G[V_2 \cup V_3]$ with an extra vertex $v_2$ adjacent to every vertex in $V_2$.
elements.sent-2411-078: If the above holds, then $(V_0,V_1,V_2,V_3)$ is a join of $G_c$ and $H_1$ and $H_2$ is a decomposition of $G_c$ induced by the join $(V_0,V_1,V_2,V_3)$.
elements.sent-2411-079: A graph $G$ is said to be j-inseparable if it does not contain any join.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Circular-arc graphs and its normalized models
elements.sent-2411-081: Given a circular-arc model~$R$ of a circular-arc graph~$G$ such that all arcs in~$R$ have pairwise distinct endpoints, Hsu distinguishes five possible relationships on a pair of arcs $u_1$ and $u_2$ in $R$, shown in Figure~\ref{fig:arcs_relation .}
elements.sent-2411-083: Definition [page 414, lines -14 to -5] Let $G$ be a circular-arc graph containing neither similar pairs nor D-vertices.

elements.sent-2411-085: Hsu showed that, if $G$ contains neither similar pairs nor D-vertices, every circular-arc model of $G$ can be turned into a normalized one by possibly extending some of its arcs.
elements.sent-2411-086: Figure~\ref{fig:ca_graph_transformation .(A) shows an exemplary circular-arc graph and Figure~\ref{fig:ca_graph_transformation .(B) presents one of its normalized model.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Consider a circular-arc graph $G$ with an N-model $R$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Associate with each arc in $R$ a~chord that connects its two endpoints.
elements.sent-2411-089: The resulting chord model $D$ is called an associated chord model for $G$.
elements.sent-2411-090: Since two arcs cross in $R$ exactly when their corresponding chords in $D$ cross, this chord model gives rise to the circle graph $G_c$.
elements.sent-2411-091: See page 420, lines -15 to -10, and Figure~\ref{fig:ca_graph_transformation for an illustration.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Next, Hsu lists some properties of the chord models $D$ of~$G_c$ obtained from the normalized models $R$ of~$G$.
elements.sent-2411-093: For every vertex $u \in V(G)$ let $I_{u}$ be the set of vertices not adjacent to $u$ in $G_c$, and let \begin{equation}{rcl} L_u = \{ v \in I_u: \quad $N(v) \subsetneq N(u)$ \quad or \quad $u$ and $v$ are strongly adjacent\}, R_u = \{ v \in I_u: \quad $u$ and $v$ are disjoint \quad or \quad $N(u) \subsetneq N(v)$\}. \end{equation} Hsu notes that for every $u \in V$ the chords representing the vertices from the set $L_u$ are on one side of $D(u)$ and the chords representing the vertices from the set $R_u$ are on the other side of $D(u)$.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}{rcl} L_u = \{ v \in I_u: \quad $N(v) \subsetneq N(u)$ \quad or \quad $u$ and $v$ are strongly adjacent\}, R_u = \{ v \in I_u: \quad $u$ and $v$ are disjoint \quad or \quad $N(u) \subsetneq N(v)$\}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-094: Based on this observation, Hsu assumes the following definition: Definition [page 424, lines 18 to 20] A chord model $D$ of~$G_c$ is conformal if for every vertex $u$ of $G$ the chords associated with vertices in $L_u$ are on one side of the chord of $D(u)$ and those associated with vertices in $R_u$ are on the other side of the chord of $D(u)$.
elements.sent-2411-095: Then, Hsu proves the following: Theorem 5.6 [page 424, lines -18 to -17] Let $G$ be a circular-arc graph [...].

elements.sent-2411-097: In Hsu's work the description of the structure of the conformal models of $G_{c}$ is split into three parts, corresponding to the cases when $V(G_{c )$ is a series, neighbourhood, or parallel module in the modular decomposition tree of $G_{c}$.}

elements.sent-2411-154: Since both of them do not follow the description given by Hsu (it is claimed in~\cite{Hsu95 that only series children of $V(G)$ might be not consistent), the graph~$G$ proves the statement of Counterexample~\ref{count:main_counter .}}
elements.sent-2411-155: The graph $G$ also shows that, assuming Hsu's partition into ``consistent modules'', Lemma~6.5 and Theorem~6.6 from~\cite{Hsu95 are also false.}
elements.sent-2411-156: In the case where $V(G_c)$ is a parallel module, the description given by Hsu is incomplete (and also not correct).
elements.sent-2411-157: In particular, Hsu constructs so-called ``consistent module tree'' for each component $C$ of $G_c$ basing on the consistent modules (T-submodules) of the component $C$.
elements.sent-2411-158: As is written in \cite{Hsu95 (see page 435, lines 27 to 29): We shall first construct a consistent decomposition tree~$T$ for the subgraph~$G_c[C]$ and then insert the vertices of $K$ into conformal models of appropriate T-submodules.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Each component~$C$ of $G_c$ is either a neighborhood or a series module in $G_c$.
elements.sent-2411-161: In the case when $C$ is a neighborhood component in $G_c$, the consistent modules (T-modules) of~$C$, as we have seen in the previous section, are not determined correctly by Hsu.
elements.sent-2411-162: Nowhere in~\cite{Hsu95 we found how Hsu defines consistent modules (T-modules) for series components of~$G_c$.. \bibliographystyle{plain.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: Sci., 15(1), 157--182 (2013)], Curtis, Lin, McConnell, Nussbaum, Soulignac, Spinrad, and Szwarcfiter showed that Hsu's isomorphism algorithm is incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-164: In the submitted paper, we show that the other two results -- the decomposition trees and the recognition algorithm -- are also flawed.
elements.sent-2411-165: We are also attaching a paper that uses the same approach as Hsu and solves the problems considered by Hsu (with the exception of the recognition algorithm).
elements.sent-2411-166: An earlier version of this paper was rejected by the SIAM Journal on Computing (SICOMP manuscript \#M131760 and \#M131760R).
elements.sent-2411-167: We intend to publish the results of this work in another journal.
elements.sent-2411-168: However, the main results presented in the attached article are already claimed, albeit with errors, in Hsu's work.
elements.sent-2411-169: To date, there has been no correction indicating that these results -- particularly the description of the structure of normalized models of circular-arc graphs -- are incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-170: We would like to emphasize that our work was rejected because one of the reviewers claimed it was too similar to Hsu's work, despite our detailed report of the errors in Hsu's work provided in the appendices.
elements.sent-2411-171: In our opinion, it is important and appropriate to rectify which results in Hsu's work and which parts of his work are incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-172: Thank you for your time and consideration.
